steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The phrase "fellow OECD member nation Australia" uses the country name as a restrictive appositive — it specifies which nation is meant, so no punctuation should separate "nation" from "Australia."

steps.title: Match to choices
steps.content:
The rule: a restrictive appositive that identifies which one must not be set off by any punctuation.
  • Choice A — "nation Australia.": no punctuation between "nation" and "Australia" — the name sits directly against the noun it identifies. ✓
  • Choice B — "nation: Australia.": a colon introduces a list or explanation after an independent clause — "in fellow OECD member nation" is not an independent clause, so a colon is incorrect. ✗
  • Choice C — "nation-Australia.": a hyphen joins words into a compound modifier, not an appositive; this construction is grammatically wrong here. ✗
  • Choice D — "nation, Australia.": a comma signals a non-restrictive appositive, implying "nation" already identifies a unique referent — but here the name is needed to specify which member. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
